elements.sent-2411-001: For positive integers $r > \ell \geq 1$, an $\ell$-cycle in an $r$-uniform hypergraph is a cycle where each edge consists of $r$ vertices and each pair of consecutive edges intersect in $\ell$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show that for $\ell \geq 2$, a random $r$-uniform hypergraph contains a Hamilton $\ell$-cycle with high probability whenever the expected number of such cycles tends to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-003: Moreover, for $\ell = 2$, we show that the normalized number of Hamilton $2$-cycles converges to a lognormal distribution.
elements.sent-2411-004: This determines the exact threshold for the appearance of non-linear Hamilton cycles in random hypergraphs, confirming a conjecture of Narayanan and Schacht.
elements.sent-2411-005: The problem of determining the critical density at which a specific substructure will appear inside a random structure has been central to the development of probabilistic combinatorics.
elements.sent-2411-006: Starting with the case of fixed size subgraphs in random graphs, a fairly complete answer was determined by Bollob\'as using the second moment method \cite{B:81 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: The cases of spanning structures have been more difficult to handle, with a general partial answer provided by Riordan \cite{R:00 , also by a careful second moment calculation.}
elements.sent-2411-008: A breakthrough work of Park and Pham \cite{PP:24 resolved the Kahn--Kalai conjecture, which gives a general purpose tool to locate these thresholds up to logarithmic factors.}
elements.sent-2411-009: However, pinning down the sharp location of thresholds still remains a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-010: Several substructures of interest have also been studied specifically.
elements.sent-2411-011: One of the most notable cases is that of perfect matchings.
elements.sent-2411-012: Early works of Erd\H{o s and R\'enyi \cite{ER:66 determined the threshold for the appearance of a perfect matching in a random graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the corresponding problem for hypergraphs, known as Shamir's problem, was notoriously difficult before it was resolved in a breakthrough work of Johansson, Kahn, and Vu \cite{JKV:08 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: We study the problem of determining when a random hypergraph contains another spanning structure of interest, the Hamilton cycle -- that is a cycle that uses every vertex of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-015: This falls into a long line of work searching for spanning structures in random (hyper)graphs.
elements.sent-2411-016: Starting with the case of graphs, foundational works by P\'{o sa \cite{P:76 , Koml\'os and Szemer\'edi \cite{KS:83 , and Ajtai, Koml\'os, and Szemer\'edi \cite{AKS:85 pinned down not only the sharp threshold, but also the more precise hitting time result equating the appearance of a Hamilton cycle with the time at which the minimum degree becomes two.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In the setting of hypergraphs, there is more than one way to form a Hamilton cycle.
elements.sent-2411-018: In particular, for any positive integers $r > \ell \geq 1$, we can consider the $r$-uniform $\ell$-cycle, where each edge consists of $r$ consecutive vertices and any two consecutive edges overlap in exactly $\ell$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-019: The case when $\ell = 1$ is known as a linear, or loose, cycle whereas when $\ell = r-1$ the cycle is called tight.
elements.sent-2411-020: Building on work of Dudek and Frieze \cite{DF:11, DF:13 , Narayanan and Schacht \cite{NS:20 showed that the first moment threshold is sharp for the appearance of any non-linear Hamilton cycle.}}
